author: @jamesross1003
text: I made something like this only instead of TENG, I used a speaker placed int the middle of a wing(flush with the wing surface as well).  Attached ribbons to the wind cutting edge.  When wind would blow across the wing it made a pressure difference that the ribbons followed making them smack against the wings body and the speaker.  It worked, not much voltage or amperage but it did generate enough to be read by the meter.  The pressure difference alone was enough to be readable, but the ribbons went mad in the wind so produced somewhat more.  You might try attaching ribbons to your front edge and run the ribbons inside the container and out the back.  The wind passing through it would move the ribbons against your TENG.  Just a thought.

author: @CNCmachiningisfun

I would like to suggest that you set your DVM to manual, as opposed to auto-ranging. 
 This will fix those annoying indecision moments that you are seeing on the voltage display.

author: @taylorwestmore4664
text: Seems like a TENG could benefit from taking a page out of the Ion Power Group research use of carbon nanotubes that increase surface area. Another technique involves using an electret insulated wire to act as a dielectric barrier to ions. Instead of collecting atmospheric ions with the wire, an insulation layer that is a strong permanent electret, causes the insulated wire to be polarized radially, the wind will do work against the ions caught in the electric double layer around the strong static field, to drive a much higher voltage and current than the tribo-electric voltage bias alone. Other experiments conducted by independent researchers show that when a dielectric electret insulated wire has a significant static voltage, the electron and ion density naturally present in the air begins to increase around the insulation, forming electrostatic double-layers in the air, which act as a plasma state, variable capacitive element in series with the insulated wire. These capacitive double-layers of plasma can store and release energy, but it may be more useful to allow them to form by accumulation and dielectric breakdown of air molecules, to greatly expand the cross-sectional area of the circuit with no added weight. The plasma double layer will intercept wind and ambient ions like a virtual sail, driving the capacitive layers together and apart the same way the reed vibrates due to mechanical restoring force, the electrostatic restoring force will determine the layers resonant frequency. The insulated wire with such a static dusty plasma field extending into the surrounding air is extremely sensitive to wind, vibration, atmospheric ion currents, heat and light and as a result can efficiently convert them to useful power. The size of the plasma field would be limited only by the dielectric constant of the insulation layers necessary to prevent breakdown at high voltage.

author: @Marian_Martin_Morgenstern
text:
Wind harp.

Make 2 strips of a thin, ideally strong material, painted just like the 2 pieces of paper. Something like long strips of scotch tape, with the conducting ink painted on one side.
No set them in to a wind harp instead of the string,with a little gap in between. 
The force of the wind and the length&tension of the tape determines the strength and frequency of the vibrations.
I'm sure that WILL make some good Volts

replies.author: @taylorwestmore4664
replies.text: I like this idea. A wind harp would be much more suitable to adapt to the plasma double-layer idea outlined in my comment about the Ion Power Group research. Even better, a cubic array of such wind harp wires should gain additional efficiency at scale, in the same way that vertical axis wind turbine arrays do.
